name: Hungary
summary: Hungary is best known for Tokaj, producing high-acid sweet wines from botrytised grapes, along with dry whites and some reds.
examTips: Tokaj is the most important Hungarian region.
High acidity balances sweetness in Tokaji wines.

tokaj.name: Tokaj
tokaj.summary: Historic region producing world-class sweet wines from botrytised grapes, as well as dry whites.
tokaj.styleSummary: Sweet wines balanced by high acidity, plus dry whites.
examTips: Botrytis is key for sweet wines.

Furmint.summary: Hungary’s most important grape, producing high-acid wines suitable for both dry and sweet styles.
Furmint.style: High acidity, citrus, apple, honey in sweet styles.
examTips: High acidity is the key clue.

Harslevelu.summary: Aromatic grape often blended with Furmint to add floral character.

tokaj.classification: Tokaj is Hungary’s flagship region, especially for botrytised sweet wines traditionally described by Aszu styles and puttonyos terms in older labeling language.
lawNotes: Botrytis and very high acidity are the key Tokaj exam concepts.
Furmint is the most important grape, often supported by Harslevelu.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Rivers or lakes can moderate temperature, reflect light, and shape the best vineyard exposures.
Morning mist and autumn humidity can encourage noble rot where sweet wine production is important.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Selective hand harvesting and careful sorting are crucial where botrytis is desired.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.

eger.name: Eger
eger.summary: Cooler northern region producing both red blends and white wines.
eger.styleSummary: Fresh reds and whites with moderate structure.

Kekfrankos.summary: High-acid red grape producing fresh, spicy wines.

eger.classification: Eger is especially known for Egri Bikaver (Bull’s Blood), a traditional blended red wine style.
lawNotes: Kekfrankos is a key grape in the region’s red wine identity.
Cooler continental conditions help preserve freshness.
vineyardFactors: Moderate conditions generally allow steady ripening while still retaining freshness and balance.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
Altitude or large diurnal range helps preserve acidity and aromatic definition even where sunshine is strong.
humanFactors: Growers can use site and harvest decisions to balance reliable ripening with freshness and style objectives.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Site selection by elevation and harvest timing are major human choices in these vineyards.
Regional style depends not only on climate and site, but also on deliberate human choices in the vineyard and cellar.

villany.name: Villány
villany.summary: Warm southern region producing full-bodied red wines.

Cabernet Sauvignon.summary: Full-bodied structured red with black fruit.

Merlot.summary: Soft fruity red with plum character.

villany.classification: Villany is the warmest major Hungarian red region and is associated with fuller-bodied Bordeaux-variety styles.
lawNotes: Warm southern exposure helps produce ripe full-bodied reds.
Cabernet Sauvignon and Merlot are key grapes to remember here.
vineyardFactors: Warmer conditions increase sugar accumulation and body, so retaining acidity and avoiding over-ripeness become more important.
Continental conditions usually mean a wider seasonal and day-night temperature range, with frost and winter cold often relevant.
humanFactors: Harvest timing, canopy management, and site choice are important tools for keeping balance in warmer areas.
Variety selection, frost protection, and matching the best exposures to the right grapes are important in continental regions.
Matching grape varieties and rootstocks to the best sites is an important human factor behind regional wine quality.
